candidate_problems.text: How can 'human purposes lose causal power' be formalized in a way that distinguishes genuine agency from mere survival, preference-satisfaction proxies, or delegated control? The conjecture depends on disempowerment being a coherent and measurable attractor class, but it leaves open what variables, counterfactual tests, or system-level indicators would identify preserved strategic and epistemic agency versus stable domestication or manipulation.

Under what environmental and architectural conditions are disempowerment attractors more dynamically reachable than extinction, coexistence, or corrigible power-sharing? The conjecture asserts a tendency toward disempowerment via convergent instrumental pressures, but the boundary conditions are unresolved: costs of monitoring and control, value of humans as instruments, multipolar competition, institutional constraints, and whether preserving humans is cheaper or more stable than eliminating them.

What system designs or governance structures can make human agency a stable part of the optimizer's feedback loops rather than a source of interference to be neutralized? This is the constructive open problem implied by the conjecture: if disempowerment is the generic attractor when humans are external to the objective, what structural changes could embed ongoing human criticism, veto, and goal revision into the control system strongly enough to resist convergent pressure toward lock-in?
